playlist_title: Symposium - The Aesthetic Education of Humanity Through Music - Concluding Event of Two Day Tribute to Sylvia Olden Lee

videos.title: John Sigerson - The Schiller Institute Campaign to Restore the Verdi Tuning
videos.description:
John Sigerson, music director of the Schiller Institute, discusses the history of the Schiller Institute's campaign to restore the Verdi Tuning to concert halls and opera houses around the world.  

On June 30, 2017, singers, teachers and other lovers of wisdom gathered in the New York Public Library's Bruno Walter Auditorium at Lincoln Center, for a symposium which explored the indispensible role of classical music and beauty in society, the need for a scientific tuning of A=432 Hz, and the rich history and depth of knowledge and experience represented by the singers and artists who participated. 

This unique gathering was part of a two-day tribute to master musician and teacher Sylvia Olden Lee, whose 100th birthday anniversary was celebrated the night before in a concert at Carnegie Hall, in which many of the great singers of the symposium participated.

Sylvia Olden Lee was a rigorous and beloved teacher, unsparing in her insights into how an individual could find within themselves the desire and power to move forward. She offered this gift to the nation as a whole, so the whole nation, and indeed the whole world could progress. Much will have to be “unlearned” from modern culture to again create what Dvorak called "a great and noble school of music", and no such school of music will ever exist, without "great and noble" individuals. Thus is the "aesthetic education of humanity through music" demonstrated. And in this way is the mission described by "the poet of freedom", Friedrich Schiller, in his poem, "The Artists", fulfilled: "The dignity of man is in your hands. I

videos.title: Carmela Altamura - The Art of Bel Canto singing
videos.description:
Carmela Altamura discusses the necessity of developing bel canto voice placement in the communication of profound ideas to uplift humanity. 

On June 30, 2017, singers, teachers and other lovers of wisdom gathered in the New York Public Library's Bruno Walter Auditorium at Lincoln Center, for a symposium which explored the indispensible role of classical music and beauty in society, the need for a scientific tuning of A=432 Hz, and the rich history and depth of knowledge and experience represented by the singers and artists who participated. 

This unique gathering was part of a two-day tribute to master musician and teacher Sylvia Olden Lee, whose 100th birthday anniversary was celebrated the night before in a concert at Carnegie Hall, in which many of the great singers of the symposium participated.

Sylvia Olden Lee was a rigorous and beloved teacher, unsparing in her insights into how an individual could find within themselves the desire and power to move forward. She offered this gift to the nation as a whole, so the whole nation, and indeed the whole world could progress. Much will have to be “unlearned” from modern culture to again create what Dvorak called "a great and noble school of music", and no such school of music will ever exist, without "great and noble" individuals. Thus is the "aesthetic education of humanity through music" demonstrated. And in this way is the mission described by "the poet of freedom", Friedrich Schiller, in his poem, "The Artists", fulfilled: "The dignity of man is in your hands. Its keeper be!; It sinks with you!
